comment_text: While his tricks are amazing, what I don't like about Mr Pearlman is that he straight up lies about "reading people." Most of his tricks are just that - magic tricks, and very good ones - but he tries to distance himself from "magic" and persuade us that he has perceptive abilities that he simply doesn't have - which could be forgiven if this was all just for entertainment, but then he also tries to sell us his self-help book, and his services to to companies, sports teams, etc, based on those lies - which makes him a bit of a scam artist. Did you note that his one tip - how to remember people's names - had nothing at all to do with his tricks, or really, anything at all? In the end, his advice on "reading people" and being "successful"  is just rehashed pop psychology. He's a very good entertainer - but that's all he is, nothing more, despite his claims otherwise - which makes him a less honest one than many of the other amazing entertainers out there.

comment_text: The New Psychic Scam: “I Read People” Psychics didn’t disappear — they just rebranded the scam. Instead of claiming supernatural powers, modern mentalists say things like: “I read microexpressions.” “I use psychology.” “I pick up subtle cues.” It’s the same old cold-reading and guesswork, wrapped in scientific-sounding language. The costume changed — the con didn’t. “I read people” is intentionally vague and impossible to prove or disprove. It creates a believable backstory for tricks anyone can learn with practice — the same tricks psychics used for decades. And those little disclaimers (“for entertainment only”) don’t protect the audience. They only protect the performer. The moment someone sees something that looks impossible, they believe it’s real, no matter what the fine print says. The show contradicts the disclaimer every time. That’s why this new version of the scam is so effective. It feels rational, it sounds intelligent, and it convinces people they’re witnessing superhuman intuition instead of stagecraft and manipulation. It’s not a breakthrough in psychology. It’s not a rare gift. It’s just an old psychic trick with better marketing.
